speaker: interviewer
text: --four.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm with my Uncle Martin, who was going to go to Paris. So he's taking me to Paris. We had to take a bus through the Russian zone. And after much adventures, which really don't have to go in here, we didn't get to Paris because there were some problems in going to Paris. But we went to a displaced-persons camp, called [NON-ENGLISH], where he rented a-- kind of a condominium. And that's where I had my bar mitzvah. It was an exciting time for me for my bar mitzvah because I could ask them what I could eat. And I requested a big weinerschnitzel with rice, which, to this day, is one of my favorite staples.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was still in Hungary and getting ready to leave. She left Hungary in the-- in a wheat barge afterwards. She came out to Canada separately. And she came out faster.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when my Uncle Martin and Eva, they wanted to go onto Canada, they could not take me because it was going to be too expensive. So they dropped me off at this orphanage, where, incidentally, my wife-- my mother visited me as an aunt because I couldn't tell them that I had a mother because I was there as an orphan. What was the name of this orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. .

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the name of the town. It was a beautiful place. From there, they could not get me to Canada because I had scarring on my lungs. But they could get me into the States. I finally-- through Munich, we, we got a Flying Tiger line, which took us to-- all the way to LaGuardia, stopping off at the Azores, where our engine caught fire. And while the plane was making an emergency landing, I was so-- became so religious. But as soon as we got out of the plane, I ate bacon and eggs, I must admit. [LAUGHS] But then we got up again. And then we made another crash landing in Saint John's in Newfoundland. Then we came in on a tail of a hurricane in LaGuardia, where they took me to the children's home, to the orphanage. And before they could-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] before they could adopt me, there was a very substantial family came with their Lincoln to adopt me. And everybody got so excited. They said, Schwartz, look. You're getting adopted. And you're going to go to Denver. Look at the Lincoln they have. Oh, you're going to have a great time, and everything. And I went to the president of the orphanage. And I said-- I confessed to her that I had a mother, and please, do not send me back to Europe. She's told me that we are not going to send me back to-- me back to Europe because it's too expensive. But we're going to send you to Toronto to your mother, where she is. I got to the Union Station in Toronto. I get there with all my baggage. And my family was waiting for me in a big crowd. My family was substantial at that time. And they didn't recognize me because I was so fat. Because in New York they gave me a little card. They said, it's a refugee boy. So whenever this little refugee boy went near a hot dog stand, they gave him free hot dogs. When I got into a delicatessen, they gave me free corned beef sandwiches.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I became so fat that my family didn't recognize me. I had to scream at them at the Union Station. Finally, they looked at me like this, oh, Tom, my god, what happened to you? So this is how I met my family again. And the, the-- it was a wonderful reunion. I lost my weight on one of my first crash diets. And at night, I went to school in Harvard Collegiate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went from Harvard Collegiate, I went to the University of Toronto. And then from the University of Toronto, I went to Hebrew Union College. And four years before that, I married my wife, Leslie Shore. We have two children. And with-- after Hebrew Union College, we had Ilana at Hebrew Union College. When I went to school there, we were-- we had her at [? Florida ?] and Fifth with the Lamaze method.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ilana is your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ilana is my daughter, who is now 25.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went for my first cantorial position in Temple Israel in Memphis, Tennessee, where Mark was born. And I was-- for five years, I was in Memphis, Tennessee. And I also played racquetball in 1973 with Elvis Presley, OK? I just wanted to tell you that we did that. Then, from Memphis, Tennessee, my father-in-law, he invited me back to Toronto to take part in his insurance business. Also, at the same time, I was cantor at Temple Emmanuel for 10 years. Then I took a sabbatical for a while. And now I'm the cantor of Temple Sinai. And presently, my children are in Guelph University. And Mark is 22 now. And Ilana is 25. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And thank God I'm here. It's sheer luck I tell you. It's luck. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about your war experiences a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not often. I just talked about the pleasant parts, you know, the onions. I talked about the, the part when I fell into the honey bin, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just those things, those interesting things. I didn't talk about the unpleasant things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you think your experiences affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously, I'm sure it affected me. Where hardships don't phase me, hunger doesn't bother me, although I clean my plate all the time. And I'm-- I'm very appreciative of food because we had so little for such a long time. And for a young boy of eight and nine, you know, to have no food is one of the most-- strongest memories in his life. So therefore, that's why I'm a little heavy [LAUGHS] lately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has your experiences affected the way you brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure it has. I'm sure it has. I may have been a little bit tough on Mark, not as tough on Ilana. I always tell them that they have to eat their food. They can't waste anything. I'm sure-- I'm sure I'm very frugal in many things, not only in material things, but also in mental giving and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] emotional openness. I'm frugal. But, but when somebody wants it, I can always give it. But it, it-- the, the war has closed me down a lot. It's, I mean, those are the things that I can imagine that have affected me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has it affected the way you believe religiously?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My, my belief is always the same as it was. I always believe in God. I believe that God is a-- is a-- is a-- God is a friendly god, who is a funny god. He's-- He-- He-- He is so big, that He doesn't really know-- He-- He's aware that you're there. But, but you have to help him, you know, so that He can guide you. In other words, my imagination of God is, is a little bit different than most people. To me, God is like a big brother who's always there, not always watching you. In other words, you could actually die. And he'll say, oh, oh, he's dead. Oh, that's too bad. It's, it's-- to Him, it's nothing, you know, if, if a human being dies. It's because there's so much for Him to-- anyway, that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it your religious belief in God that sustained you through the camps? Or if-- what did sustain you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother sustained me a lot. And also, the belief in God. My belief in God was much, much stronger than it is right now. It, it was much more basic. God was much more real for me through the camps, and so on. I, I, I always thought that people died because they didn't do, do the right thing for God. Even though that they looked innocent, there must have been something there. So I have a very primitive view of God. But that has been modified since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cantor Schwartz, why did you want to give this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is very important to me that people should realize what happened and that we human beings are capable of such despicable and unbelievable cruelty to each other. And I want people to know this so that they can guard against this in the future, and that they may be-- they can cure the whole milieu of human beings of these type of feelings that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that we are capable of. I don't think it is possible to do it. Yet, maybe in the next two centuries, perhaps it will happen. But until then, you have these atrocities. You know, just like in Sarajevo-- and you have it in Rwanda. And these people killing each other, like, I mean, it's unbelievable. Well, I told you what happens. After you kill 25 or 50 people, it's already-- it's-- you're used to it. And it's like nothing. It's-- that's what we have to be cured of because there's a streak in us that-- that can awaken this unspeakable cruelty. I-- this is the thing that I want people to realize and to guard against.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still nightmare about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one nightmare. It's not really a nightmare. It's a pleasant type of a thing. It's-- we're running in our cattle cars again. And the cattle car stops. And we all come out. And the cattle car changes into a fish-- into a fish. And it's-- and then somebody opens the fish. And it's cooked. And it's lox. [LAUGHS] And somebody yells to get some bagels. And [LAUGHS] it's-- that's what it is. And I have this occasionally. But it's, it's not a pleasant dream. It's-- I must-- it's a weird dream. And I'm always scared to death. It's-- it's hard to believe. But that's what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would your final message be to the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, is to [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] have-- try and feel what, what the other person feels when you're doing something to him or when you talk to him. Have empathy. I think that's-- I think that's one of the most important things. Do unto others what you-- and others-- [LAUGHS] do not do unto others what you would do badly. In other words, that's what I want. Empathize with other people. Once you know how the other people feel-- other person feels-- you can't possibly go wrong. And that's what we don't do because, because it's very hard to do it. And it's a heavy thing to do. It's, it's a lot of work. And we're lazy. Inside, we are lazy, you know. It's-- you're-- we're lazy when it has to do with emotional things. When it has to do with relationships, we're lazy. We're lazy to talk. We're lazy to bring it up. We're lazy to confess to ourselves and to confess to our person who's close to us. We're lazy. We're afraid. And that's because we are human. And that's my message.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you very much for allowing us into your home and for sharing with us your experiences. I understand that you have a special contribution that you'd like to make at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have a-- since I'm a cantor, I have a song to sing. And most of these-- most cantors would sing 'El Maleh Rahamim,' which is the right thing to do, which is a dirge-- and for the dead. But I'm going to sing something Hungarian, a song which was sang and composed by a famous Hungarian Rabbi called the Rabbi of Kalev. And he sings about this bird. Of course, it's the Messiah. He sings about this wonderful bird that will come someday. And he will change the world where everybody will know what the other person is thinking. And he sings about the Messiah. And this is how, how the song goes. [SINGING 'SZOL A KAKAS MAR'] This is a snapshot of my mother and father. It's-- was probably done in 1935. And my father's name is Marton Schwartz. And my mother's name is Lola Schwartz-- wonderful people, wonderful picture. This is a picture-- strange-- in Szarvas, which is north of Totkomlos, where my mother from left to right, is on the bicycle with Uncle Miklos, who just died a couple of years ago. And this is Aunt Lilly on the far right, who was without-- with us in the concentration camp. Too bad we don't have Uncle Harry's picture here right now. That's old Caruso Schwartz in Naples, Italy, letting loose with a note about 1962 in Via Posillipo in Naples. I was studying-- I was studying singing in Italy then. I was doing opera. And I was going to be an opera singer. But my mother got ill. I had to come back to Canada. And that sealed my fate to the cantorate. This is my grandmother and grandfather, whom I loved very much. I still love them. It's June, 1957. They are looking out the door off 1002 Spadina Road, where Uncle Harry lives now. And, oh, my, my grandfather died when he was 85. My grandmother died at 99. Here we go. This is the 1963 marriage picture of Tom Schwartz and Leslie Schwartz. From left to right, it's Steve Korda, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. Then my grandfather, Sam Katz. Then comes my grandmother, Malvina Katz, then yours truly, Tom Schwartz, then Leslie Schwartz. Then comes Eva Korda, Martin Korda, who are still living here in Toronto. Then comes Irene Korda and Ben Korda, who are presently in Montreal. And that's our big wedding picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my gorgeous daughter, Ilana. It was taken two years ago in 1992, approximately. Isn't she stunning? This is my son, Mark Schwartz, the bodybuilder. Would you believe it? This was taken at the CNE grounds, where he won his weight class. He's going to school now in Guelph, where he's working hard on sciences with my beautiful daughter Ilana, who's also working on sciences and is doing wonderfully.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Here we go. This was taken a year and a half ago. My wife, Leslie, the gorgeous person who's taking care of the life insurance office, and that real [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] executive type on her right, who's me, Tommy Schwartz. And below, the skin of the family-- is that how you say it-- is Fred [? Shore, ?] Leslie's father. Here we go. This is a shot two years ago at Maple Downs Golf Club. From left to right, that's my son, Mark. Then comes Ilana. And that person laughing at you in that bozo shirt is yours truly, Tommy Schwartz. Then comes my father-in-law, who's like my father to me, Fred [? Shore. ?] Then comes [? Ann ?] [? Shore, ?] five-times Maple Downs Club champion in golf, no less-- a fabulous athlete and a tremendous competitor. And there is her daughter, Leslie Schwartz, my wife, who looks gorgeous in such a beautiful summer day.
